questions.question: What happens at the Link Layer of the quantum protocol stack?
questions.explanation: The Link Layer handles entanglement generation between directly connected (neighbor) nodes. It manages heralding protocols and link-level error correction to provide entangled pairs to the Network Layer.

questions.question: What is the primary function of a Repeater node?
questions.explanation: Repeater nodes extend entanglement distance by performing entanglement swapping. They connect to two neighbors and can join their separate entangled pairs into one longer-distance pair.

questions.question: How does a Router node differ from a Repeater node?

options.text: Routers connect multiple paths and make routing decisions

questions.explanation: Router nodes have multiple interfaces and can make routing decisions to direct entanglement through different paths. Repeaters typically have only two interfaces and simply extend a single path.

questions.question: Why is classical communication essential at every layer?

options.text: Heralding, coordination, and classical processing are needed
Quantum communication doesn't work without it

questions.explanation: Classical communication is essential for heralding (announcing successful operations), coordination between nodes, routing decisions, and post-processing. Most quantum protocols require both quantum and classical channels.

questions.question: What layer handles end-to-end entanglement through multiple hops?
questions.explanation: The Network Layer handles multi-hop entanglement distribution through routing, entanglement swapping, and purification. It uses Link Layer services to provide end-to-end entanglement to the Transport Layer.

questions.question: What makes quantum routing different from classical routing?

options.text: Entanglement is consumed and cannot be copied or stored indefinitely

questions.explanation: Unlike classical data that can be copied and stored, entanglement is consumed when used and degrades over time. This means quantum routing must consider timing, fidelity decay, and resource availability differently than classical routing.

questions.question: The quantum internet can use store-and-forward routing like classical networks.
questions.explanation: False. Store-and-forward routing copies data at each hop, which is forbidden by the no-cloning theorem for quantum states. Instead, quantum networks use entanglement swapping to extend entanglement without copying quantum states.
